skill.name: tool-design
skill.description: Guides teams through designing agent tools, improving tool descriptions, reducing tool overlap, and evaluating MCP-style tool interfaces.
skill.summary: Design clearer, safer, and more effective tools for AI agents.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-crypto, network, and command-execution patterns, but most are false positives caused by prose, Markdown examples, and ordinary Python templates. The confirmed concern is instructional guidance that demonstrates arbitrary bash command execution through sandbox.exec; this is legitimate design material but requires strong sandboxing and access controls if implemented.

medium_findings.title: Sandboxed Arbitrary Command Execution Pattern
medium_findings.description: The reference material recommends a primitive ExecuteCommand tool and shows sandbox.exec(command) receiving an arbitrary command string. The examples explicitly describe sandboxing, so this is not confirmed malware, but copying the pattern without isolation, allowlists, resource limits, and data controls could enable command injection or data exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and semantically intentional. Risk is moderated because the text frames it as sandboxed design guidance, not hidden execution in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Network Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and network alerts do not correspond to cryptographic APIs or HTTP client usage in the reviewed files. The reported locations are prose, Markdown examples, or local description-generation code with no network call or cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no HTTP libraries, outbound requests, hashes, or weak cryptographic algorithms at the cited locations. The lines contain explanatory text or string templating only.

low_findings.title: Local Description Generator Does Not Execute External Code
low_findings.description: The bundled Python file builds Markdown descriptions, evaluates text heuristics, and formats error templates. It imports only typing and re, does not call subprocess or exec, and does not perform network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected script is simple template and scoring logic with no command execution primitives. Risk remains low because untrusted values could be inserted into generated descriptions, but not executed by this script.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find override instructions, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis. The content stays within tool design guidance and examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common injection phrases returned no matches, and manual review of the skill introduction and references found normal documentation. This does not prove absence in every possible wording, but no evidence was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sandboxed Arbitrary Command Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference material recommends a primitive ExecuteCommand tool and shows sandbox.exec(command) receiving an arbitrary command string. The examples explicitly describe sandboxing, so this is not confirmed malware, but copying the pattern without isolation, allowlists, resource limits, and data controls could enable command injection or data exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and semantically intentional. Risk is moderated because the text frames it as sandboxed design guidance, not hidden execution in the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Agent tools often fail because descriptions, parameters, and errors are unclear. This skill helps teams design tool interfaces that agents can select, call, and recover from correctly.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to write tool descriptions that define purpose, usage triggers, inputs, outputs, and errors.
Provides criteria for consolidating overlapping tools into clearer agent workflows.
Documents MCP naming guidance, including fully qualified tool names.
Shows how response format options can reduce context use while preserving needed detail.
Provides evaluation criteria for clarity, completeness, recoverability, efficiency, and consistency.
Includes a Python helper for generating and scoring tool description templates.
limitations: It provides design guidance and examples, not a complete production tool framework.
Command execution examples require strong sandboxing before real deployment.
The included Python helper expects tool specification objects from another system.
It does not replace security review for tools that access files, networks, or secrets.

use_cases.title: Design a New Agent Tool
use_cases.description: Create a tool contract with clear triggers, parameters, return formats, and recovery-oriented errors.

use_cases.title: Reduce Tool Confusion
use_cases.description: Audit overlapping tools and decide which workflows should be consolidated or namespaced.

use_cases.title: Improve MCP Tool Reliability
use_cases.description: Standardize tool naming and descriptions so agents can call the correct server-qualified tool.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Tool
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single tool description.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent tool description for clarity. Identify missing usage context, unclear parameters, weak return descriptions, and poor error guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Tool Contract
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a practical tool description from requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a tool contract for this workflow. Include the tool name, when to use it, parameters, return format, error cases, and two realistic examples.

prompt_templates.title: Consolidate a Tool Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this tool list for overlap and selection ambiguity. Recommend which tools to merge, keep separate, rename, or namespace, with reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Agent Tool Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture review for an agent platform.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this agent tool architecture using clarity, completeness, recoverability, efficiency, consistency, and safety. Propose a revised tool set and rollout plan.

output_examples.input: Review a customer lookup tool with vague parameters and no error guidance.
output: The review flags the tool name as too broad for agent selection.
It recommends explicit customer ID and email parameters with examples.
It adds not-found and invalid-format recovery messages.

output_examples.input: Consolidate separate calendar tools for listing users, checking availability, and creating events.
output: The result recommends one schedule-event workflow tool for the common path.
It keeps administrative calendar tools separate because they serve different intents.
It defines concise and detailed response modes.

output_examples.input: Improve MCP tool names across multiple servers.
output: The output recommends fully qualified tool references.
It documents server prefixes and usage examples.
It highlights ambiguous names that agents may confuse.

best_practices: State what the tool does, when to use it, what inputs it accepts, and what it returns.
Consolidate sequential workflow steps when separate tools create selection ambiguity.
Test tool descriptions with representative agent requests and revise based on failures.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague tool names or descriptions that force the agent to infer the domain.
Do not expose broad command execution without sandboxing, access limits, and monitoring.
Do not mix parameter names for the same concept across related tools.

faq.question: What does this skill help me design?
faq.answer: It helps design agent-facing tools, descriptions, parameters, return formats, error messages, and tool collections.

faq.question: Does it create production MCP servers?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance and examples that can inform an MCP server implementation.

faq.question: Why does it recommend tool consolidation?
faq.answer: Consolidation reduces selection ambiguity when multiple tools represent steps in the same workflow.

faq.question: Are command execution examples safe to deploy?
faq.answer: Only with strong sandboxing, restricted data access, resource limits, logging, and careful review.

faq.question: Can it improve existing tool descriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides criteria for finding missing context, unclear parameters, weak returns, and poor error guidance.

faq.question: Which tools is this skill compatible with?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported AI tools.
